channel.title: Drupal Core
channel.description: Stay informed about developments in Drupal Core.

item.title: #3573869: Remove deprecated code from Views and Views UI
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: a71a3ed · +103/-960 prod · +7/-66 test · +0/-18 infra · 100 files
  • Discussion: #3573869 · 5 contributors · 25 comments

Summary

Drupal 12 removed code that was deprecated in Drupal 11 from the Views and Views UI modules. This cleanup effort removes outdated functions, methods, and patterns that were marked for removal in the next major version. The change affects multiple areas including template preprocessing functions, deprecated service methods, hook implementations, and internal utility functions. Site owners using Drupal 12 will benefit from a cleaner codebase with less technical debt, though any custom code still calling deprecated functions will need updates.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • API change: Removed deprecated functions views_entity_field_label(), views_field_default_views_data(), _views_field_get_entity_type_storage(), and template_preprocess_*() functions for Views templates
  • Deprecation: Removed previously deprecated methods including EntityViewsData::getFieldStorageDefinitions(), HandlerBase::defineExtraOptions(), and views_ui_contextual_links_suppress() functions

Technical details

The cleanup removes code deprecated across Drupal 11.x releases. Major removals include standalone template_preprocess_*() functions that were moved to hook classes like ViewsThemeHooks and ViewsUiThemeHooks. These preprocessing functions are now registered directly in hook_theme() implementations.

The change removes utility functions like views_entity_field_label() (replaced by the entity field manager service), views_field_default_views_data() (replaced by the field data provider service), and contextual links suppression functions that are no longer needed.

Constructor parameters that were previously optional in ViewExecutable and ViewExecutableFactory are now required, specifically the display plugin manager. The code also removes support for the deprecated theme_file definition in plugin definitions and removes deprecated template files views.theme.inc and views_ui.theme.inc.

Filter operators now throw a LogicException instead of triggering a deprecation when a filter doesn't implement FilterOperatorsInterface. Template documentation updates replace references to deprecated functions with their hook class equivalents. The config updater no longer triggers deprecations for automated fixes that should have been applied manually. One test that verified deprecated behavior was removed entirely.

Upgrade

If custom code calls removed functions, replace them with the documented alternatives:

// Old: Deprecated in Drupal 11.2
$label = views_entity_field_label($entity_type, $field_name);

// New: Use the service
$label = \Drupal::service('entity_field.manager')->getFieldLabels($entity_type, $field_name);

// Old: Deprecated in Drupal 11.2
$data = views_field_default_views_data($field_storage);

// New: Use the service
$data = \Drupal::service('views.field_data_provider')->defaultFieldImplementation($field_storage);

For Views plugins, remove any theme_file definitions from plugin annotations. Ensure filter plugins implement FilterOperatorsInterface if they define custom operators.

Template documentation should reference hook class methods instead of removed functions (e.g., \Drupal\views\Hook\ViewsThemeHooks::preprocessViewsView() instead of template_preprocess_views_view()).

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 16, 2026
  • Committed: March 14, 2026 (25 days later)

Key contributors

  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • sourav_paul (Innoraft)
  • dcam
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

This issue was opened in February 2026 with the straightforward goal of removing deprecated code ahead of Drupal 12. An initial attempt by sourav_paul had too many overlapping changes, so smustgrave opened a fresh merge request to provide a cleaner review surface. dcam provided strategic guidance to keep the scope manageable by recommending difficult edge cases be moved to follow-up issues, preventing delays and rebases on this large but mechanical change. The focused approach allowed the team to get the bulk of deprecation removals committed within a month. sourav_paul acknowledged smustgrave's help in pushing the issue forward during their project commitments.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3550083: robots.txt should disallow all '/search/' and '/search?' pages by default
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 519d5d6 · +8/-0 · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3550083 · 10 contributors · 37 comments

Summary

Search engines were indexing dynamically generated search result pages on Drupal sites, especially those using query parameters like /search?q=keyword. This caused crawlers to index poor quality content and follow infinite combinations of faceted search pages, degrading site performance and SEO. The default robots.txt file already blocked path-based search URLs like /search/keyword, but did not block query parameter searches. The updated robots.txt now blocks both patterns, protecting sites from excessive crawler activity on search pages. This only affects new installations and sites that use scaffolded files.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The default robots.txt file included Disallow: /search/ with a trailing slash, which blocked URLs like /search/node but did not block /search?title=robots. According to Google's robots.txt specification, the pattern /search/ matches paths that continue after the trailing slash but does not match when a query string immediately follows.

Sites using Search API, Views-based search, or faceted search typically generate URLs with query parameters rather than path segments. Crawlers would index every possible combination of facet filters, creating performance issues and polluting search engine indexes.

The fix adds two new disallow rules: Disallow: /search? and Disallow: /index.php/search?. These specifically block URLs where query parameters immediately follow the search path. The original /search/ rules remain in place to continue blocking path-based search URLs. Both the root robots.txt and the scaffold version in core/assets/scaffold/files/robots.txt were updated.

Because robots.txt is a scaffolded file that sites may customize, existing installations will not automatically receive this update.

Upgrade

Existing sites should manually update their robots.txt file if they want to block crawler access to search pages with query parameters. Add these two lines to the file:

Disallow: /search?
Disallow: /index.php/search?

Place them near the existing Disallow: /search/ rules for consistency. If you have customized your robots.txt file or use a different search path, adjust the rules accordingly. New installations and sites that regenerate scaffolded files will automatically receive these updated rules.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 3, 2025
  • Committed: March 13, 2026 (5 months later)

Key contributors

  • bserem (Annertech)
  • poker10 (ActivIT s.r.o.)
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

This issue took five months from opening to commit as it worked through the implications of changing a site-owner-managed file. The initial proposal suggested replacing the existing rule entirely, but poker10 identified that removing the trailing slash would also block content with URL aliases starting with /search. This led to the current approach of adding new rules rather than replacing existing ones. The issue required framework manager sign-off because it modifies scaffolded files. After catch confirmed the approach and requested a change record and release notes, bserem created both and the issue moved to commit. longwave made a final commit to the fork before godotislate committed it to both main and 11.x branches.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3568387: Move text_summary to TextSummary service and deprecate
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: acf3e5f · +322/-210 prod · +28/-26 test · 12 files
  • Discussion: #3568387 · 6 contributors · 44 comments

Summary

Drupal core is modernizing its text processing utilities by moving functionality from procedural functions into services. The text_summary() function, which generates trimmed summaries of text content, has been deprecated. This function is widely used to create automatic excerpts of content when a manual summary is not provided. The new service-based approach provides better dependency management and follows current Drupal architectural standards. This is part of an ongoing effort to eventually remove the text module's .module file entirely by eliminating procedural code.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: The text_summary() function is deprecated; use the TextSummary service instead.

Technical details

The new TextSummary service encapsulates the same logic previously in the text_summary() procedural function. The generate() method accepts the same parameters: text content (now typed as string|\Stringable), an optional text format ID, and a character length limit (now required as int, defaulting to 600).

The service is autowired and depends on EntityTypeManagerInterface to load filter format entities. The implementation remains identical to the original function: it checks for the <!--break--> delimiter, truncates to the specified length, attempts to break at paragraph or sentence boundaries, and normalizes HTML when appropriate filters are enabled.

Core usages in NodeTokensHooks and TextTrimmedFormatter have been updated to call \Drupal::service(TextSummary::class)->generate(). The deprecated text_summary() function now serves as a thin wrapper that triggers a deprecation notice and delegates to the service.

The new service uses strict types and proper type hints. The third parameter $size is now required as int in the service signature, though the deprecated function maintains backward compatibility by reading from text.settings configuration when NULL is passed.

Upgrade

Replace calls to text_summary() with the TextSummary service:

// Before:
$summary = text_summary($text, $format, $size);

// After:
$summary = \Drupal::service(TextSummary::class)->generate($text, $format, $size);

For dependency injection:

class MyClass {
  public function __construct(
    protected TextSummary $textSummary,
  ) {}

  public function process($text, $format, $size) {
    return $this->textSummary->generate($text, $format, $size);
  }
}

Note that the third parameter $size should now be passed as an integer. Previously, passing NULL would read from text.settings configuration, but the new service defaults to 600 characters if omitted.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 19, 2026
  • Committed: March 12, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • dcam
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • mstrelan
  • godotislate

Collaboration

This issue took nearly two months from opening to commit, with 44 comments discussing implementation details. The path was not straightforward, involving several key design decisions.

Early in the process, contributors debated whether to make the new API internal. Berdir argued against it, noting that the function is used in contrib and has value as a public API. The team decided to keep it public.

A significant discussion centered on type hints. Initial attempts to add strict types caused test failures when FilteredMarkup objects were passed instead of strings. Berdir suggested accepting string|\Stringable, which resolved the issue while properly documenting behavior.

Another debate concerned the $size parameter. Berdir proposed making it required in the new service (removing the NULL default that read from config) to simplify the API going forward, deferring config cleanup to a separate issue #3436677. This approach made the new service cleaner while maintaining backward compatibility in the deprecated function.

Nicxvan made the key contribution of converting from a static helper class to a proper service with dependency injection, improving testability and architectural consistency.

Lessons learned

  • When deprecating a procedural function in favor of a service method, avoid reading configuration with implicit defaults in the new API. Require all parameters explicitly at the service boundary, and keep configuration-reading logic in the deprecated wrapper for backward compatibility. This makes the new API clearer and allows removing configuration dependencies later. (via berdir, nicxvan)
  • When adding type hints to a new service that replaces existing code, use string|\Stringable instead of just string for text parameters that may receive markup objects. This prevents type errors when FilteredMarkup or similar objects are passed, which is common in Drupal's rendering system. (via berdir)
  • In kernel tests, use Drupal::service() instead of $this->container->get() to retrieve services. After a container rebuild, $this->container can point to outdated objects, while Drupal::service() always retrieves from the current container. (via godotislate)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3547353: Move HIDDEN, CLOSED and OPEN constants in CommentItemInterface to new CommentingStatus enum
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 979d45e · +344/-272 prod · +296/-304 test · +24/-0 infra · 157 files
  • Discussion: #3547353 · 5 contributors · 42 comments

Summary

The comment system now uses a modern enum to represent whether comments are open, closed, or hidden. This replaces older numeric constants with clearer, type-safe code that is easier to understand and maintain. The change makes comment status values consistent with how other parts of Drupal handle options. Sites and modules can continue using the old constants during the transition period, but should update to the enum for future compatibility.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: Constants CommentItemInterface::HIDDEN, CommentItemInterface::CLOSED, and CommentItemInterface::OPEN deprecated in favor of CommentingStatus enum cases.
  • New API: New CommentingStatus enum with Hidden, Closed, and Open cases provides type-safe comment field status values.

Technical details

Three class constants on CommentItemInterface (HIDDEN, CLOSED, OPEN) have been deprecated and replaced with a new CommentingStatus enum. The enum defines three cases: Hidden (0), Closed (1), and Open (2), matching the original constant values for backward compatibility.

Throughout core, status checks now compare against enum cases rather than integer constants. The enum uses OptionsEnumTrait to provide translatable labels and an asOptions() method for form element options. Widget and filter classes use CommentingStatus::asOptions() instead of manually building option arrays.

Most code reads the status value from the field and converts it to an enum case using CommentingStatus::tryFrom(), then compares that case. When writing values, code uses ->value to get the integer representation. The CommentTestTrait::addDefaultCommentField() method signature now accepts either a CommentingStatus enum case or an integer for the default value parameter, checking the type and converting integers to enum cases.

The interface constants remain but are marked deprecated with @see tags pointing to the change record. They will be removed in Drupal 13.0.0.

Upgrade

Replace references to CommentItemInterface::OPEN, CommentItemInterface::CLOSED, and CommentItemInterface::HIDDEN with the new enum:

// Before
use Drupal\comment\Plugin\Field\FieldType\CommentItemInterface;
$status = CommentItemInterface::OPEN;
if ($entity->comment->status == CommentItemInterface::CLOSED) { }

// After
use Drupal\comment\CommentingStatus;
$status = CommentingStatus::Open->value;
if (CommentingStatus::tryFrom((int) $entity->comment->status) == CommentingStatus::Closed) { }

For form element options:

// Before
'#options' => [
  CommentItemInterface::OPEN => $this->t('Open'),
  CommentItemInterface::CLOSED => $this->t('Closed'),
  CommentItemInterface::HIDDEN => $this->t('Hidden'),
],

// After
'#options' => CommentingStatus::asOptions(),

When creating entities or fields with a default status value, pass the enum case's value property or use the enum directly where supported.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: September 18, 2025
  • First commit: March 10, 2026 (5 months later)
  • Last commit: March 12, 2026 (1 day later)

Key contributors

  • mstrelan
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • godotislate

Collaboration

This issue was opened on 2025-09-18 and committed in four installments over two days in March 2026, six months later. The initial work moved three integer constants to a new enum. The MR required multiple rebases due to conflicts with ongoing core development, including fixes to long line declarations and changes to test structure.

A key challenge emerged after the first commit: the 11.x branch had deprecated modules already split to contrib that still used the old constants. Rather than fixing those deprecated modules, the team added a new PHPStan baseline to ignore those deprecation warnings, which godotislate successfully committed after some iteration on the approach. This pragmatic solution avoided changing modules that were already removed from 11.x development.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571994: Reduce container rebuilds in functional tests
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: c84deae · +48/-8 · 2 files
  • Discussion: #3571994 · 3 contributors · 11 comments

Summary

Functional tests now run significantly faster by avoiding unnecessary container rebuilds during installation. Previously, the installer would rebuild the dependency injection container 11 times in a simple test, even when no modules or themes had changed. This optimization reduces that to 8 rebuilds by tracking which extensions were compiled and only rebuilding when modules or themes actually change. Test suite execution times improved by 30-50% in optimal conditions, making local development and continuous integration faster.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The InstallerKernel class previously forced a container rebuild every time initializeContainer() was called, even when no extensions had changed. Since allowDumping is FALSE during installation, no cached container exists, so the parent class would always recompile from scratch.

The fix adds a compiledExtensions property that tracks which modules and themes were present during the last compilation. Before triggering a rebuild, the kernel now checks if the current extensions match the compiled ones by comparing counts and keys. If they match and a container already exists, it reuses the existing container. Only when extensions actually differ does it set containerNeedsRebuild to TRUE and call the parent initialization.

This reduces container rebuilds from 11 to 8 in a trivial functional test. While actual wall-clock time varies due to hardware availability, the optimization shows measurable improvements in test runtime, with minimum times dropping from 3:34 to 1:47 in some test runs.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 6, 2026
  • Committed: March 11, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

Collaboration

This optimization was identified and implemented by longwave, who provided detailed performance measurements across multiple pipeline runs to validate the improvement. The challenge was balancing performance gains against the complexity of test scenarios. An initial approach tried more aggressive combining of rebuilds, but longwave found this wouldn't work reliably in complicated test setups. The final approach focused on the clear win of avoiding duplicate InstallerKernel rebuilds. Catch reviewed and moved to RTBC, noting that reducing the performance floor was valuable despite hardware variance making exact measurements difficult. The change was committed to both main and 11.x branches.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3577684: uninstall-contact.php doesn't delete entity displays
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: ea25dee · +5/-1 · 1 file
  • Discussion: #3577684 · 3 contributors · 10 comments

Summary

A test fixture that simulates uninstalling the Contact module was incomplete, leaving behind form and view display configuration for contact messages. This caused any update tests using the ConfigEntityUpdater to update display configurations to crash with a "contact_message entity type does not exist" error. The fixture now properly removes all display-related configuration when uninstalling Contact, preventing these test failures.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The uninstall-contact.php test fixture simulates uninstalling the Contact module by removing its database entries and configuration. However, it only deleted config entries matching contact.%, which missed the entity form and view display configurations stored as core.entity_form_display.contact% and core.entity_view_display.contact%.

When tests using ConfigEntityUpdater tried to process all form or view displays, they attempted to load display configs for the contact_message entity type. Since the entity type had been removed but the displays remained, this caused a PluginNotFoundException.

The fix updates the delete query to use an OR condition group that matches all three config name patterns: the original contact.% pattern plus the two display patterns. This ensures the fixture completely removes all Contact-related configuration, matching what happens during actual module uninstallation.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 7, 2026
  • Committed: March 11, 2026 (4 days later)

Key contributors

  • dcam
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

The issue was reported on March 7, 2026 when dcam discovered that rebasing another issue (#2865710) caused all update tests to fail. The root cause was quickly identified as missing display entity deletions in the test fixture. The fix was straightforward, requiring only a small change to add the missing config name patterns to the deletion query. The issue was committed on March 11, 2026, just four days after being opened.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3564589: Prepend path to error log in js_testing_log.js
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: e632f53 · +14/-14 · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3564589 · 4 contributors · 22 comments

Summary

When JavaScript errors occur during functional JavaScript tests, Drupal now includes the URL path where the error happened in the test failure message. This makes it much easier to debug tests that visit multiple pages, since developers can immediately see which page caused the error instead of having to guess or add extra debugging code. The error messages now show the path followed by a pipe character and then the error details.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The js_testing_log.js file now prepends window.location.pathname to both JavaScript errors and warnings before storing them in session storage. The format is path|message, for example /js_errors_test|Error: A manually thrown error.

The DrupalSelenium2Driver class needed updates to parse deprecation warnings correctly. Since the path prefix comes before the [Deprecation] marker, the code now uses str_contains() instead of str_starts_with() to detect deprecation messages, and uses a regex to extract the deprecation text after the marker.

Test expectations in JavascriptErrorsTest were updated to expect the path prefix in error messages. The Nightwatch assertion for deprecation errors also received a similar regex update to strip the path prefix before matching messages.

This change only affects the test framework's internal logging format. No production code or public APIs are impacted.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: December 22, 2025
  • Committed: March 11, 2026 (2 months later)

Key contributors

  • danflanagan8
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3347842: Deprecate the trusted data concept in configuration
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: a038e2b · +178/-148 prod · +470/-478 test · 180 files
  • Discussion: #3347842 · 8 contributors · 40 comments

Summary

The configuration system previously allowed code to bypass schema validation by marking data as "trusted." This optimization was added years ago to speed up site installation, but it created maintenance problems. Code had to check whether data was trusted and handle sorting and validation differently depending on the answer. This made the system fragile and harder to reason about.

With this change, all configuration saves now apply schema validation and normalization. The trusted data parameter is deprecated but still accepted for backward compatibility. The system is now more consistent and predictable. Tests that need to write invalid configuration for upgrade path testing can use a new test trait instead of the deprecated API.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The $has_trusted_data parameter on Config::save() and the trustData() method on config entities are now deprecated. Internally, the system still respects the flag when present to maintain backward compatibility, but all new code should assume schema validation always runs.

During saves, configuration is now normalized according to schema even when coming from module installation or configuration sync. This ensures data is properly sorted and typed. The original performance concern (repeated schema cache rebuilds during install) has been largely mitigated by multi-module installation improvements that reduce cache rebuilds.

The Views module benefited from a side effect: it no longer loads all action configuration entities just to check if any exist for a given entity type. The bulk form field plugin now registers for all entity types with a base table, not just those that already have actions defined. This reduces query counts on cold cache page loads.

A new RawConfigWriterTrait provides a testing API for upgrade tests that need to write configuration without schema validation. It writes directly to storage and invalidates caches, bypassing the entity system entirely.

The hasTrustedData() method is not yet deprecated because only three modules use it. It will be deprecated in Drupal 13 when it can only return FALSE, allowing a simpler upgrade path for contrib.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 14, 2023
  • Committed: March 10, 2026 (2 years later)

Key contributors

  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • andypost (Skilld)
  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • borisson_ (Calibrate)
  • bircher (Nuvole)
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

This issue was opened by alexpott in March 2023 to address a code smell in the configuration system. The "trusted data" concept was originally introduced to improve performance during site installation, but it created fragility and forced workarounds in other parts of the codebase.

The change sat for nearly three years before serious work began. Once active development started in early 2026, it moved quickly through review. Multiple committers and reviewers (wim leers, andypost, bircher, borisson_, catch) contributed to refining the approach. A key decision emerged late in the process: rather than deprecate hasTrustedData() immediately, the team decided to wait until Drupal 13 after the ability to trust data was removed, making it easier for contrib modules to upgrade incrementally.

The MR revealed unexpected benefits: removing trusted data eliminated unnecessary action configuration loads in Views, reducing query counts in performance tests. The change was committed to main in March 2026 and backported to 11.x within hours.

Lessons learned

  • When configuration data is normalized and validated on save, duplication of normalization logic across entities becomes fragile. If some code paths skip normalization (via a 'trusted data' flag), each entity must handle normalization separately even when schema already defines the canonical form. Normalize consistently at one layer rather than conditionally at multiple layers. (via alexpott)
  • When performance optimizations skip validation during bulk operations like installation or import, configuration can become denormalized over time as schemas evolve or relationships change. Even if data was normalized when saved, schema changes or new related entities can introduce noise. Validate and normalize on every save to maintain consistency, accepting the performance cost. (via alexpott, donquixote)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575792: Bump PHPStan & friends to latest release
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 878ea54 · +2/-1 prod · +2/-2 test · +267/-69 infra · 13 files
  • Discussion: #3575792 · 3 contributors · 15 comments

Summary

Drupal core has updated its PHPStan static analysis tool and related extensions to their latest versions. PHPStan is a development tool that helps find bugs in code before running it. The update includes support for property hooks, a new PHP 8.4 feature. This change improves the quality of automated code analysis during Drupal development. The update also revealed additional code issues that were added to the baseline for future cleanup.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This issue updates PHPStan from version 2.1.38 to 2.1.40 in the main branch (11.x received a similar update). It also updates mglaman/phpstan-drupal to 2.0.11, which adds support for parsing PHP 8.4 property hooks, and phpstan/phpstan-phpunit to 2.0.16. The phpstan-deprecation-rules extension was updated to 2.0.4 as well.

The update exposed new errors that PHPStan now detects. These were added to the PHPStan baseline file rather than being fixed immediately. The new errors include warnings about variables in empty() checks that always exist and are always falsy, missing return type specifications on several methods, and missing generic type specifications for certain interfaces.

One quirk encountered in the 11.x branch: PHPStan incorrectly reported that non-capturing catch blocks were unsupported, even though Drupal requires PHP 8.3+. This was worked around by adding a variable to the catch block and suppressing the corresponding PHPCS error.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 25, 2026
  • Committed: March 11, 2026 (13 days later)

Key contributors

  • mondrake
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3538177: Remove jQuery UI _focusedElement and _focusTabbable from dialog Ajax
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 62adbdf · +72/-4 · 2 files
  • Discussion: #3538177 · 6 contributors · 29 comments

Summary

Drupal's ongoing effort to replace jQuery UI Dialog takes another step forward. This change removes the last jQuery UI-specific focus management code from AJAX dialog handling and replaces it with native browser methods. The dialog system now handles keyboard navigation and focus management using standard JavaScript instead of jQuery UI's internal methods. This brings Drupal closer to removing the jQuery UI Dialog library entirely, reducing page weight and improving maintainability. The focus behavior remains identical for site visitors and developers.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

When AJAX commands open or update dialogs, Drupal needs to move keyboard focus into the dialog for accessibility. Previously this relied on jQuery UI Dialog's internal _focusedElement and _focusTabbable() methods.

The new implementation uses vanilla JavaScript and Drupal's existing focusable() utility (which wraps the tabbable library) to replicate the exact same focus behavior. It follows a priority order: first try elements with the autofocus attribute, then the first focusable element in the dialog content, then focusable buttons in the button pane, then the close button, and finally the dialog container itself.

This change only affects dialog.ajax.js, which handles AJAX responses that manipulate dialogs. The jQuery UI Dialog widget itself remains in place for now. The focus logic is now entirely self-contained instead of calling into jQuery UI's private API, making it easier to eventually swap in a native HTML <dialog> element or alternative widget without changing the AJAX command layer.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: July 26, 2025
  • Committed: March 11, 2026 (7 months later)

Key contributors

  • finnsky (Skilld)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • nod_ (Très Bien Tech)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This issue was opened in July 2025 as part of the broader jQuery UI Dialog replacement initiative. The patch went through several iterations to address test failures in DialogTest. A key discussion point emerged around how this differed from existing jQuery UI overrides in dialog.jquery-ui.js. The contributor clarified that while Drupal already overrode _focusTabbable() to use the tabbable library, this change moves to pure vanilla JavaScript, setting up for eventually replacing the entire jQuery UI widget. After manual testing in Views UI (a complex dialog-heavy interface), the change was committed to both main and 11.x branches in March 2026. A follow-up concern was raised about potential random test failures, noted after commit.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3285176: Warning message links to "available updates" even if user does not have permission for that page
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 420765c · +68/-28 prod · +94/-0 test · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3285176 · 10 contributors · 62 comments

Summary

After Drupal added a "View update notifications" permission, users with that permission could see security update warnings that included a link to the Available Updates page, even when they lacked permission to view that page. Clicking the link resulted in an access denied error. This fix checks whether the current user can access the Available Updates report before including a link. Users without access now see the warning text without a broken link. Email notifications continue to include the link since they may be sent to users without accounts on the site.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Update module generates warning messages in two contexts: on-screen messages via hook_page_top() for the current user, and email notifications sent to arbitrary addresses. The _update_message_text() function now accepts a $is_email parameter that controls whether to perform access checks and use absolute URLs.

For on-screen messages, the code now calls Url::access() before including a link to the Available Updates report. If the current user cannot access update.status route, the message displays plain text instead of a hyperlink. The UpdateRequirements::requirementCheck() method applies similar logic when appending the "See the available updates page" sentence.

For email messages, the new parameter bypasses access checks since recipients may not have accounts on the site. Emails always include absolute URLs to the report. This preserves existing behavior for email notifications while fixing the user experience for users with limited permissions viewing on-screen warnings.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 10, 2022
  • Committed: March 10, 2026 (3 years later)

Key contributors

  • benjifisher (FRUITION)
  • dww (TEN7)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • feyp (werk21)
  • larowlan (PreviousNext)
  • ravi.shankar (OpenSense Labs)
  • acbramley (PreviousNext)
  • godotislate
  • aaronmchale
  • andregp
  • rkoller
  • shaal
  • simohell
  • worldlinemine

Collaboration

This issue was opened in June 2022 after the Drupal Usability Meeting noticed the permission gap introduced by #332796. The fix required careful iteration over multiple code paths. Early patches checked specific permissions, but reviewers pushed for using Url::access() to future-proof the solution. The challenge was handling both on-screen messages (where access checks matter) and email notifications (where they don't). After the initial RTBC in July 2022, the issue went dormant when larowlan raised code style concerns about nested conditionals. It took until March 2026 for dww to convert it to a merge request and simplify the logic with early returns. The final version properly separates email from on-screen contexts.

Lessons learned

  • When generating messages that include links, check whether the current user has access to the link destination before including it. For UI messages shown to the current user, use URL access checks to conditionally format messages with or without links. For emails sent to arbitrary recipients where access cannot be determined, include links unconditionally since you cannot reliably check permissions for unknown users. (via dww)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3549730: Enable LongLineDeclaration
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: b36ea26 · +621/-125 prod · +14/-3 test · +1/-22 infra · 69 files
  • Discussion: #3549730 · 6 contributors · 21 comments

Summary

Drupal's coding standards include a rule that flags arrays with long declarations, but this rule was not enforced across the entire codebase. This change fixes all remaining violations of the LongLineDeclaration standard by splitting long array declarations and function calls across multiple lines for better readability. The code now consistently follows the rule that lines should not exceed a certain length, making the codebase easier to read and maintain. All changes preserve existing functionality while improving code formatting.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Drupal.Array.Array.LongLineDeclaration PHPCS sniff was previously enabled only for specific directories through include patterns in phpcs.xml.dist. This issue removed those directory restrictions and enabled the sniff globally across the entire codebase.

The changes address violations by splitting long lines in several ways: breaking array declarations across multiple lines with each element on its own line, splitting function calls with multiple parameters, and wrapping chained method calls. In batch.inc, an unnecessary array_merge() call was removed and the parameters were passed directly instead. One line in responsive_image was marked with a PHPCS ignore comment because reformatting would harm readability.

The formatting changes follow existing patterns in the codebase, prioritizing readability and consistency. No API behavior changes occurred. The diff touches approximately 170 files across core, affecting function calls with long parameter lists, array declarations, and method chaining patterns.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 1, 2025
  • Committed: March 10, 2026 (5 months later)

Key contributors

  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • dcam

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571143: Convert BlockHooks::pageTop to attachment in BlockController::demo
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: c3a8de2 · +40/-86 · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3571143 · 4 contributors · 13 comments

Summary

Drupal previously checked every page request to determine whether to display a "back" link on the block region demonstration page. This created unnecessary overhead for all page loads. The change moves this logic directly into the block demonstration controller, so the check only runs when actually viewing that specific page. This improves performance by eliminating a route check on every request.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The BlockHooks::pageTop() method was being invoked on every page request via hook_page_top(). Inside this hook, it checked whether the current route was block.admin_demo before conditionally adding a "back" link to the page. This meant every request performed a route match check even though the vast majority of requests were not for the block demo page.

The change removes this hook implementation entirely. Instead, the logic now lives directly in BlockController::demo(), which only executes when visiting the block region demonstration page. The backlink is now attached using #attached['page_top'], a capability added in #3339905.

The controller constructor was modernized to use promoted readonly properties and now injects ThemeManagerInterface in addition to ThemeHandlerInterface. The backlink building logic remains functionally identical but is now triggered only when needed.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 2, 2026
  • Committed: March 10, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • acbramley (PreviousNext)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • godotislate
  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)

Collaboration

This issue was opened by acbramley as follow-up work after #3339905 made it possible to attach page_top elements directly from controllers. The change was straightforward: moving existing logic from a hook to a controller method. smustgrave manually tested that the back button still appeared and functioned correctly on the block demonstration page. godotislate reviewed the merge request and confirmed that no backward compatibility layer was needed since BlockController is not extended in contrib. The issue moved from creation to commit in about five weeks.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3469143: MainContentViewSubscriber should use a service locator
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 98b2f7c · +38/-146 · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3469143 · 4 contributors · 21 comments

Summary

Drupal's rendering system has been modernized to use Symfony's service locator pattern instead of an older compiler pass approach. This internal refactoring improves code quality and reduces complexity without changing how Drupal renders content. The change removes approximately 55 lines of code while making the dependency injection pattern clearer and more maintainable. Site owners and developers will not notice any functional difference in how their sites work.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The MainContentViewSubscriber previously used a custom compiler pass (MainContentRenderersPass) to collect all services tagged with render.main_content_renderer into a container parameter. The subscriber then used the class resolver to instantiate the appropriate renderer based on format.

This has been replaced with Symfony's autowired service locator pattern. The #[AutowireLocator] attribute automatically creates a service locator containing all tagged services, indexed by the format attribute. The subscriber now receives this ServiceLocator directly and calls get() to retrieve renderers.

The constructor signature changed from accepting ClassResolverInterface, RouteMatchInterface, and an array parameter to accepting only RouteMatchInterface and ServiceLocator. The service is now autowired in core.services.yml instead of having explicit arguments.

The MainContentRenderersPass compiler pass and the main_content_renderers parameter were deleted entirely. The documentation in core.api.php was updated to reference TaggedHandlersPass as an example instead.

This modernizes the code to use Symfony best practices and reduces the amount of custom Drupal-specific dependency injection code.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: August 20, 2024
  • Committed: March 10, 2026 (1 year later)

Key contributors

  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • dcam
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

This issue was opened in August 2024 by longwave as a code quality improvement but remained uncommitted for over 18 months before being merged in March 2026. The initial patch removed 55 lines of code, demonstrating the efficiency gain from using service locators.

Early discussion focused on backward compatibility concerns, particularly whether the compiler pass deletion needed deprecation. Longwave correctly noted that Drupal's BC policy treats compiler passes as internal, so no deprecation was necessary. This decision was later confirmed by catch.

The issue required a rebase after the extended wait, but the technical approach remained sound throughout. Dcam provided valuable review feedback that was addressed before final approval.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3397041: Autowire core modules that do not require explicit configuration
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 5afd135 · +54/-177 prod · +60/-8 test · 43 files
  • Discussion: #3397041 · 7 contributors · 27 comments

Summary

Drupal core modules can now automatically find and inject most of the dependencies their services need, without requiring manual configuration. This reduces boilerplate in service definition files and makes it easier for developers to work with dependency injection. The change affects dozens of core modules, removing hundreds of lines of repetitive dependency wiring code while maintaining the same runtime behavior.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This issue enables autowiring for core module services by adding autowire: true to the _defaults section of each module's *.services.yml file. Autowiring allows the dependency injection container to automatically resolve constructor arguments based on type hints, eliminating the need to manually list each dependency in the arguments key.

The change removes explicit arguments declarations from services where all constructor parameters can be resolved by autowiring. Services that require explicit configuration (like those with scalar parameters, specific service configurations, or complex setups) retain their manual arguments.

A new test in AutowireTest::testCoreAutowiring() validates that services are properly configured for autowiring. The test parses all core service files, checks which services have constructor parameters that can be autowired, and ensures developers remove manual arguments when autowiring is possible.

This builds on the autowiring infrastructure introduced in earlier versions and follows #3295751, which enabled autowiring for core/core.services.yml. Module services follow the same pattern, making the codebase more consistent and reducing maintenance overhead when service dependencies change.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 26, 2023
  • Committed: March 10, 2026 (2 years later)

Key contributors

  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • spokje
  • godotislate
  • dcam
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

This issue was opened in October 2023 but sat dormant for over two years before being completed in March 2026. Initially postponed on #3295751 to avoid duplicate changes and reroll conflicts, the approach was eventually reversed when that parent issue encountered difficult problems. The decision to proceed independently allowed this simpler, more focused change to move forward.

Multiple contributors worked on rebases as modules were removed from core over the years. In the final stretch, dcam added extensive use of the #[Autowire] attribute and split work into per-module commits for easier review. However, longwave clarified that the "do not require explicit configuration" scope meant only handling trivial autowiring cases without attributes, keeping the issue focused. dcam reverted those commits and saved the attribute work for a follow-up, allowing the simpler change to reach RTBC and commit.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572171: Persist is_syncing across container rebuilds
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: c376f87 · +39/-42 prod · +24/-15 test · 9 files
  • Discussion: #3572171 · 4 contributors · 13 comments

Summary

During configuration import, Drupal sets a flag to prevent certain automatic actions like creating default blocks when themes are installed. Installing a theme triggers a container rebuild, which previously lost this flag. This caused blocks to be created during config sync even though they should have been skipped. The flag is now preserved across container rebuilds, ensuring that operations that should be skipped during configuration import remain skipped throughout the entire process.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The config.installer service maintains an isSyncing() flag that tells Drupal when configuration is being imported. Code like block_theme_initialize() checks this flag to skip creating blocks during config sync, since blocks should come from the imported configuration.

The ModuleInstaller class already saved and restored this flag when installing modules triggered a container rebuild. However, when themes are installed, the container is also rebuilt through DrupalKernel::initializeContainer(), and this path did not preserve the flag.

The fix moves the preservation logic from ModuleInstaller::updateKernel() into DrupalKernel::initializeContainer(). Now both module and theme installation use the same mechanism. Before rebuilding the container, the syncing status and source storage are captured. After the new container is built, these values are restored.

The test was updated to actually install a theme (test_theme) instead of manually invoking hooks with hardcoded values. This provides a more realistic test that catches regressions in the theme installation path.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 8, 2026
  • Committed: March 10, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

This issue was opened by nicxvan to address a regression in #3182716 where theme installation during config sync incorrectly created blocks. Berdir quickly implemented a fix by moving the syncing flag preservation from ModuleInstaller into DrupalKernel, making it work for both module and theme installation. The test was improved to actually install a theme rather than just invoking hooks. Phenaproxima reviewed and appreciated the solution for fixing the regression while making the extension system more consistent.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3578028: Update underscore.js to 1.13.8
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 11.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 8bc541e · +18/-18 prod · +30/-9 infra · 15 files
  • Discussion: #3578028 · 6 contributors · 13 comments

Summary

Drupal core's bundled Underscore.js library was updated from version 1.13.7 to 1.13.8 to address a security vulnerability. The previous version contained a denial-of-service (DoS) flaw where deeply nested data structures could trigger stack overflow errors in the .flatten and .isEqual functions due to uncontrolled recursion. The vulnerability was rated high severity (CVSS 7.5–8.2) and is fixed in the upstream 1.13.8 release. This update protects sites from potential service disruptions caused by malicious input to these functions.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Security: Underscore.js updated to 1.13.8 to fix CVE-2026-27601, a high-severity DoS vulnerability in .flatten and .isEqual functions

Technical details

The vulnerability (CVE-2026-27601, CWE-770) existed in Underscore.js versions prior to 1.13.8. The .flatten and .isEqual functions performed recursion without depth limits. An attacker could craft deeply nested structures (e.g., via JSON.parse without validation) and pass them to these functions, causing stack overflow and crashing the JavaScript runtime.

The 1.13.8 release addresses this by implementing depth controls in the affected functions. The update was applied to Drupal core by running yarn vendor-update to pull the latest minified library and its source map. The fix is entirely within the vendored JavaScript library; no Drupal code changes were required.

The vulnerability affects the .flatten function when called without a finite depth parameter and the .isEqual function when comparing attacker-controlled deeply nested objects. Sites using these functions on untrusted input were at risk of DoS attacks. The update mitigates the risk by preventing unbounded recursion.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 9, 2026
  • Committed: March 10, 2026

Key contributors

  • alfaro
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

This critical security issue was opened and addressed within one day. The original report by alfaro identified the vulnerability and proposed the straightforward solution of updating the library. longwave implemented the fix within hours by running Drupal's standard vendor update process and creating a merge request. The change was reviewed and committed to all supported branches (11.x, 10.4.x, 10.3.x) on the same day, demonstrating the rapid response to a high-severity third-party library vulnerability. The swift turnaround reflects the community's security-first approach and well-established processes for vendor library updates.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3534144: [meta] Set Drupal 12 platform and browser requirements by Jan 15 2026
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical plan
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3534144 · 5 contributors · 34 comments

Summary

Drupal 12 platform requirements have been finalized to ensure compatibility with modern hosting environments while balancing upgrade feasibility. The requirements follow established policies that select versions based on availability, stability, and support lifecycles. PHP 8.5 will be required following the policy to use the highest stable version available. Database requirements mostly remain conservative, keeping MySQL at 8.0 despite it approaching end of life, because hosts upgrade databases more slowly than other components and the security impact is low. MariaDB increases to 10.11 to follow LTS policies, and PostgreSQL jumps to version 18 released six months before the beta window.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Requirements: Drupal 12 will require PHP 8.5, MySQL 8.0 (no change), MariaDB 10.11 (up from 10.6), PostgreSQL 18 (up from 16), and SQLite 3.45 (no change), with Composer 2.9.3 recommended.

Technical details

This meta issue coordinated the selection of platform requirements for Drupal 12.0.0 by applying policies defined in separate issues. The PHP requirement of 8.5 follows #3406215, which established that new major versions should require the latest stable PHP release available at beta. Database version selection attempted to follow the draft policy in #3363102, though that policy is still being finalized.

For MySQL, the team decided to keep 8.0 as the minimum despite it being EOL before Drupal 12 releases. This reflects that database upgrades are harder for hosts than PHP upgrades, and security risks from database versions are much lower than from PHP versions. Supporting newer versions is more important than requiring them. MariaDB increased to 10.11 to align with their LTS cycle. PostgreSQL moved to version 18 based on the six-month-before-release window. SQLite stayed at 3.45 as no compelling features required a bump.

Composer 2.9.3 is recommended but not strictly required, as enforcement is difficult and Package Manager now uses local Composer when available. Browser requirements remained unchanged per existing policy.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: July 4, 2025

Key contributors

  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • xjm (Zoocha)
  • gábor hojtsy (Acquia)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This meta issue ran from July 2025 through January 2026, coordinating multiple child issues to finalize requirements before the beta window. The discussion evolved from initially suggesting more aggressive database version bumps to a more conservative approach based on practical hosting constraints. The team recognized that while policies should guide decisions, real-world upgrade difficulty matters. Database requirements generated the most discussion, with the team carefully balancing support lifecycles against adoption barriers. An announcement was drafted collaboratively and published in January 2026. Documentation updates followed across multiple system requirements pages. The Overview page was later marked obsolete to avoid maintaining duplicate information in multiple locations.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3046985: The word "Second" needs translation context
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: c8e4731 · +48/-4 · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3046985 · 17 contributors · 53 comments

Summary

Drupal now adds translation context to ordinal numbers and time units that were previously ambiguous. Words like "Second" can mean either a unit of time or an ordering position. In languages like German, these require completely different translations. Without context, the word "Sekunde" (time unit) might be mistranslated as "zweiter" (ordinal number), or vice versa. Additionally, ordinal numbers are adjectives in many languages and require different forms based on grammatical gender. Layout region labels, date format options, and similar interface text now include context strings so translators can provide accurate translations based on how each word is actually used.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The change adds translation context to all bare occurrences of ordinal numbers in core's user interface strings. Two contexts are used: "PHP date format" for time-related instances of "Second" in date element dropdowns and Views sort options, and "Layout region" for ordinal position labels in layout configuration files.

In PHP code, context is added as the third parameter to the t() function: t('Second', [], ['context' => 'PHP date format']). For YAML layout definition files, a new translation context key is added alongside each region label.

The issue focused on instances where a bare word appears without surrounding context that would clarify its meaning. Strings like "Second item" were already clear and required no changes. Only standalone words like "Second" needed disambiguation.

The change affects layout files in layout_builder.layouts.yml, layout_discovery.layouts.yml, and umami.layouts.yml, the Datelist element class, and the Views Date sort plugin. Context was added to all ordinal numbers (First through Fifth) found in layout regions, not just "Second", because these also require gender-specific translations in many languages.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: April 9, 2019
  • Committed: March 9, 2026 (6 years later)

Key contributors

  • jhodgdon
  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • hexabinaer
  • sanduhrs
  • rpayanm (Agileana)
  • joachim namyslo
  • m-schmitt
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This issue remained open for nearly seven years before resolution. The initial report in 2019 clearly identified the problem for German translation, but determining the correct approach took considerable discussion. Early questions centered on whether YAML layout files could even support translation context, leading to discoveries about how the POTX extraction process works.

The biggest challenge was defining appropriate context strings. Initial suggestions for generic contexts like "ordinal" proved insufficient because adjectives require gender matching in many languages. The solution evolved to use specific contexts describing what each ordinal refers to, such as "Layout region."

The issue was reserved for mentored contribution during DrupalCon Europe 2020. Stefan Auditor created the initial patch in late 2020 that established the pattern used in the final solution. After conversion to a merge request in 2024, the change received final review and was committed in March 2026. A follow-up issue was noted to potentially add automated checks for bare words requiring translation context.

Lessons learned

  • When a translatable string has multiple meanings that translate differently across languages, provide translation context using the context parameter in t(). Words like 'Second' (time unit vs. ordinal number) and ordinal numbers that require gender agreement ('first', 'second', 'third') need context specifying what they refer to (e.g., 'ordinal for footer region' or 'datetime') so translators can choose the grammatically correct translation for each use. (via jhodgdon, joachim namyslo)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572051: hook_icon_pack_alter() is undocumented
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 65b0772 · +45/-0 · 3 files
  • Discussion: #3572051 · 5 contributors · 26 comments

Summary

The hook hook_icon_pack_alter() was previously undocumented, making it difficult for developers to discover or use. This hook allows modules to modify icon pack definitions that are part of Drupal's core Icon API. With proper documentation now in place, developers can more easily customize icon packs provided by core or contributed modules. The documentation includes a practical example showing how to change the label of the navigation icon pack.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Icon API in Drupal core includes an IconPackManager plugin manager that discovers icon pack definitions from YAML files. Like other plugin managers, it supports an alter hook through alterInfo() to allow modifications during discovery. However, hook_icon_pack_alter() was never documented, making it effectively invisible to developers.

The new documentation was added to core/lib/Drupal/Core/Render/theme.api.php alongside other theme and rendering-related hooks. The hook receives an array of icon pack definitions keyed by pack ID and allows modules to modify any aspect of these definitions. The documentation includes a simple example that changes the label of the navigation icon pack.

Discussion during review clarified that theme.api.php was the appropriate location rather than core.api.php, since it contains other theme-related hooks regardless of whether they can only be invoked by modules or themes. A follow-up issue was opened to explore allowing themes to invoke this alter hook as well.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 6, 2026
  • Committed: March 6, 2026 (28 days later)

Key contributors

  • tstoeckler (resc.)
  • kanchanandhini6
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

Collaboration

This issue provided a straightforward first-time contribution opportunity. After initial guidance on where to place the documentation and what existing hooks to model it after, the contributor quickly produced working code. The main iteration came from a mid-review discussion about whether the hook belonged in core.api.php or theme.api.php. After examining precedent with hooks like hook_library_info_build(), the team agreed to move it to theme.api.php as more appropriate for theme-related functionality. A follow-up issue was filed to potentially expand theme support for the hook. The change was committed to all active branches within one month of opening.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.
